title: Healthcare high-risk applicability
summary: Healthcare-related AI can fall within high-risk use cases when it determines access to essential healthcare services, supports emergency healthcare patient triage, or is used for life and health insurance risk assessment and pricing.
obligations: Map the intended use to Annex III before launch.
Treat the system as high-risk when it affects healthcare access, triage, or health insurance risk decisions.
Keep evidence showing why the use case is or is not considered high-risk.
source_reference: EU AI Act Annex III point 5

title: Employment and HR high-risk applicability
summary: Employment-related AI can fall within high-risk use cases when it supports recruitment, candidate filtering, worker management, task allocation, promotion, termination, or access to self-employment.
obligations: Assess whether AI output materially affects hiring or employment opportunities.
Document the employment workflow in which the AI is used.
Preserve evidence supporting the high-risk classification decision.
source_reference: EU AI Act Annex III point 4

title: Risk management system
summary: Providers of high-risk AI should operate a documented and continuous risk-management process covering design, testing, deployment, and post-market changes.
obligations: Identify reasonably foreseeable risks to health, safety, and fundamental rights.
Define mitigation measures and evaluate residual risk.
Update the risk file when incidents, drift, or intended-use changes appear.

title: Data and data governance
summary: Training, validation, and testing data for high-risk AI should be relevant, sufficiently representative, and governed through documented collection, preparation, and quality controls.
obligations: Describe data sources, collection logic, preprocessing, and quality checks.
Assess representativeness and known limitations for the intended population.
Record bias mitigation and missing-data handling decisions.

title: Technical documentation package
summary: High-risk AI requires technical documentation that explains intended purpose, versions, interfaces, hardware or software dependencies, data inputs, operating logic, and other evidence needed for conformity assessment.
obligations: Maintain a versioned technical dossier for each AI system release.
Describe intended purpose, deployment form, dependencies, and operating logic.
Keep the dossier complete enough for regulators and internal reviewers to assess compliance.
source_reference: EU AI Act Article 11 and Annex IV

title: Record-keeping and logging
summary: High-risk AI should support logging and traceability so operators can monitor functioning, investigate incidents, and demonstrate compliance evidence.
obligations: Enable logs needed to reconstruct system operation and outcomes.
Keep records aligned with the intended use and risk posture.
Use logging outputs to support incident review and post-market analysis.

title: Transparency and instructions for use
summary: High-risk AI should be accompanied by information that allows deployers to understand capabilities, limitations, performance characteristics, and operating conditions.
obligations: Provide instructions for use and known limitations.
Explain the intended purpose, performance assumptions, and required human role.
State conditions under which outputs may degrade or require extra controls.

title: Human oversight
summary: High-risk AI should include oversight measures that enable trained people to interpret outputs, detect anomalies, and intervene or override when necessary.
obligations: Define who oversees the system and under what authority.
Provide procedures for escalation, override, and safe fallback.
Train oversight personnel on the system context and limitations.

title: Accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity
summary: High-risk AI should achieve an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity, with testing and controls proportionate to the intended use and foreseeable misuse.
obligations: Define measurable performance targets and testing evidence.
Address resilience to errors, drift, and adversarial manipulation where relevant.
Document cybersecurity safeguards and incident handling assumptions.

title: Quality management system
summary: Providers of high-risk AI should operate a quality management system covering regulatory strategy, design controls, testing, documentation, post-market monitoring, and corrective actions.
obligations: Define governance for compliance and release management.
Keep procedures for design changes, incident response, and corrective actions.
Link the quality system to technical documentation and post-market monitoring evidence.
source_reference: EU AI Act Article 17 and Annex VII

title: Personal-data processing principles
summary: Personal data should be processed lawfully, fairly, transparently, for specified purposes, with data minimisation, accuracy, storage limitation, integrity, confidentiality, and accountability.
obligations: Define the purpose of processing before using the data.
Limit data use to what is necessary for the stated purpose.
Keep evidence showing how the controller complies with the principles.

title: Lawful basis and special-category data
summary: Controllers need a lawful basis for processing, and health or other special-category data require an additional condition when processed under GDPR.
obligations: Document the lawful basis used for each processing purpose.
Identify whether the dataset contains special-category data such as health data.
Record the extra condition that permits special-category processing when applicable.

title: Transparency notices
summary: Data subjects should receive clear information about the controller, purposes, lawful basis, retention, recipients, and rights when personal data are collected or obtained indirectly.
obligations: Maintain privacy notices aligned with each intake source.
Explain retention, rights, contacts, and any automated decision logic where relevant.
Ensure notices remain understandable for affected people, including workers or patients.

title: Automated individual decision-making
summary: Individuals have protections when decisions based solely on automated processing produce legal effects or similarly significant impacts, including the ability to obtain human intervention and contest the decision where applicable.
obligations: Assess whether the AI output drives solely automated significant decisions.
Add human review where the legal threshold may be met.
Provide mechanisms for affected people to request review or challenge outcomes when required.

title: Data protection by design and by default
summary: Controllers should integrate privacy controls into system design and configure defaults so that only necessary personal data are processed for each purpose.
obligations: Review architecture decisions against data minimisation and access restrictions.
Set defaults that avoid unnecessary data collection or exposure.
Keep design evidence showing privacy controls were considered from the start.

title: Security of processing
summary: Controllers and processors should implement security measures appropriate to the processing risk, including confidentiality, integrity, availability, resilience, and testing of safeguards.
obligations: Assess processing risk and align security measures to that level.
Protect confidentiality, integrity, and availability of personal data.
Test and review safeguards over time, especially for sensitive or high-risk processing.

title: Data protection impact assessment
summary: A DPIA is required when processing is likely to result in high risk to individuals, especially for systematic evaluation, sensitive data at scale, or new technologies affecting fundamental rights.
obligations: Decide whether the use case triggers a DPIA before production use.
Describe processing operations, necessity, proportionality, risks, and mitigations.
Update the DPIA when the processing purpose or risk profile materially changes.
